---
title: "Quickstart"
---

## Install

Pick whichever fits; the install script is the quickest.

### Install script

Downloads the prebuilt binary for macOS or Linux and verifies it against the release checksums:

```bash
curl -fsSL https://m.canouil.dev/oboro/install.sh | bash
```

It installs into `/usr/local/bin` when writable, otherwise `~/.local/bin`.
Pin a version with `--version`, choose a directory with `--dir`, and pass `--help` for the rest.
The script needs `bash` and `curl`; on a minimal distribution such as Alpine, install them first with `apk add bash curl`.

### Docker

No toolchain needed; the image is one static binary on `distroless`.
The vault volume is not optional, because the placeholder mapping lives in it:

```bash
docker volume create oboro-vault
docker run --rm \
  -v oboro-vault:/vault \
  -v "$PWD":/work -w /work \
  --user "$(id -u):$(id -g)" \
  ghcr.io/mcanouil/oboro:latest clean contract.docx
```

The [Docker](docker.qmd) page covers `restore` and the volume in full.

### Prebuilt binary, by hand

Download the archive for your machine and `SHA256SUMS` from the [releases page](https://github.com/mcanouil/oboro/releases), then verify and install it:

```bash
VERSION=0.1.0
TARGET=x86_64-unknown-linux-musl   # or the row that matches your machine

curl -fsSLO "https://github.com/mcanouil/oboro/releases/download/${VERSION}/oboro-${VERSION}-${TARGET}.tar.gz"
curl -fsSLO "https://github.com/mcanouil/oboro/releases/download/${VERSION}/SHA256SUMS"
sha256sum --ignore-missing --check SHA256SUMS   # shasum -a 256 on macOS
tar -xzf "oboro-${VERSION}-${TARGET}.tar.gz"
install -m 0755 oboro /usr/local/bin/oboro
```

### With Rust already installed

```bash
cargo install --git https://github.com/mcanouil/oboro
```

### From source

Required for the optional features, which the prebuilt binary and the image do not carry:

```bash
git clone https://github.com/mcanouil/oboro.git
cd oboro
cargo build --release
```

The binary lands in `target/release/oboro`.

That plain build depends on nothing but Rust.
Two capabilities are optional and compiled in rather than shipped: reading images needs the Tesseract system libraries, and recognising names links ONNX Runtime, which the build fetches over the network.

::: {.panel-tabset}

## Names and companies

Finding names nobody told the tool about needs the recognition model:

```bash
cargo build --release --features ner   # downloads ONNX Runtime while building
oboro models pull
```

The build downloads ONNX Runtime, so `--features ner` needs network access and a fully offline build fails here.
`models pull` then fetches the model, about 348 MB once, checked against pinned hashes.
Once built, it is the only command that touches the network.

## Images

Reading images and scanned documents needs Tesseract:

```bash
# Debian and Ubuntu
sudo apt-get install tesseract-ocr tesseract-ocr-fra libtesseract-dev libleptonica-dev

# macOS
brew install tesseract tesseract-lang

cargo build --release --features ocr
```

## Both

```bash
cargo build --release --features "ner,ocr"
```

:::

### In a devcontainer

For building or contributing with nothing on the host but Docker.
The container carries the pinned Rust toolchain, Tesseract and the OCR libraries, so `cargo build --release --features "ner,ocr"` runs inside it without setting up a host toolchain, and the binary lands in `target/release/oboro` as usual.
In Visual Studio Code or a GitHub Codespace, reopen the folder in the container when prompted; [Development](development.qmd) has the manual `docker` invocation.

Check what your build can do:

```bash
oboro doctor
```

## Clean a document

```bash
oboro clean contract.docx
```

That writes `contract.clean.md` beside the input and tells you what it replaced:

```text
contract.docx -> contract.clean.md (7 replaced: ADDRESS 2, EMAIL 1, IBAN 1, ORG 1, PERSON 1, PHONE 1)
```

Read it, then paste it into whichever model you are using.

Point `clean` at a directory to do a whole folder at once; add `--recursive` to include its subfolders:

```bash
oboro clean contracts/ --recursive
```

## Decide for yourself what gets hidden

`clean` redacts everything it finds.
`review` shows you the list first and lets you reject anything it got wrong:

```bash
oboro review contract.docx
```

```text
┌ contract.docx  —  6 of 7 will be redacted ─────────────────────┐
│ [x] PERSON      31%  Jean Dupont                               │
│ [x] ORG         52%  Acme Consulting SARL                      │
│ [ ] PERSON      22%  Le Havre                                  │
│ [x] IBAN       100%  FR14 2004 1010 0505 0001 3M02 606         │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
 j/k move   space toggle   a accept all   n reject none   w write
```

Rejecting a detection leaves the value in the output and never records it anywhere.

## Bring the answer back

The model replies using the placeholders.
Save its answer and put the real values back:

```bash
oboro restore answer.md
```

Or keep it in a pipeline:

```bash
pbpaste | oboro restore /dev/stdin --stdout
```

## A whole round trip

```bash
oboro clean contract.docx --stdout | pbcopy   # paste into the model
pbpaste > answer.md                            # save what it said
oboro restore answer.md --stdout               # read it with real values
```
